content: BullMQ connections in Ovyro MUST use maxRetriesPerRequest:null (src/lib/queue/connection.ts) — BullMQ Workers issue blocking commands (BRPOPLPUSH) that outlive ioredis's default retry budget, and ioredis aborts them otherwise. The same options are used for producer-side Queues so one process-wide singleton connection serves both roles; BullMQ duplicates it internally for the blocking client. Close order matters on shutdown: obliterate/close Queues BEFORE closing the shared connection, or the queue close calls fail against a dead socket.

description: A Redis-gated integration test written as 'describe.skipIf(!available)(... startWorkers() in the describe body ...)' still boots the workers when the suite is skipped: bun (like jest/vitest) executes describe callbacks during collection even for skipped suites, but skips beforeAll/afterAll. Result: with Redis down the suite reported '4 skip' yet the log showed 'worker started' and opened Redis connections that the skipped afterAll never closed. Separately, the ioredis reachability probe crashed the run with '[ioredis] Unhandled error event' because connect() rejecting is not enough — the error event still needs a listener.
resolution: Move startWorkers() into beforeAll with 'let workers: Worker[] = []' at describe scope, and attach 'probe.on("error", () => {})' before probe.connect(). Verify the skip path explicitly by pointing REDIS_URL at a dead port (redis://localhost:6399) — a green 'N skip' line alone does NOT prove nothing booted; read the log output.

name: Worker/Next.js isolation guarded by a module-graph test
description: worker/isolation.test.ts walks the real import graph from worker/index.ts — resolving relative specifiers and the tsconfig '@/*' -> src/* alias by hand — and asserts no reachable module imports 'next', 'next/*', 'react' or 'react-*'. A grep of the worker/ directory alone is insufficient: the worker shares /src/lib per spec 8.2, so a Next.js import can arrive transitively through a shared lib module a later phase edits. The walker self-checks with expect(visited.size).toBeGreaterThan(4) so a broken resolver fails loudly instead of vacuously passing on an empty graph. Regex is /(?:from|import)\s*\(?\s*["']([^"']+)["']/g — an earlier '(?:import|export)[\s\S]*?from' version silently skipped imports by lazily scanning past them from a 'from'-less export statement.

title: Queue payload contracts land for all five spec-8 queues; processors only for implemented phases
rationale: src/lib/queue/jobs.ts declares Zod v4 schemas for email, meta-capi, media-processing, token-health, listing-expiry and system, but worker/processors/index.ts registers handlers only for email and system. Later phases add handlers without touching the contract, so producers can be written against a stable payload shape now, and an unregistered queue's jobs WAIT in Redis rather than being dropped. enqueue() parses the payload before it reaches Redis so a malformed job fails in the request that created it instead of silently burning retries in the worker; processors re-parse on the way out because a job may have been enqueued by an older deploy. 'system.echo' doubles as the end-to-end liveness job and the integration test's drain target.

name: worker-lifecycle-verified-cross-process
description: Worker verified against real Redis with a genuinely separate producer process: 'bun run worker' boots Workers for the registered queues (email, system), a second process calls enqueue('system','echo',{message}) from src/lib/queue, and the job reaches state=completed in ~250ms with the echoed returnvalue. SIGTERM produces 'shutting down' -> 'shutdown complete' -> exit 0 in ~2s with no orphan process, because stopWorkers() awaits worker.close() (drains active jobs) then closeRedisConnection(). Only email and system have processors registered; meta-capi, media-processing, token-health and listing-expiry have payload contracts but no handlers, so jobs on them queue and wait rather than being dropped. Gotcha: running redis-server from the repo root writes dump.rdb into cwd — now gitignored.

BullMQ v5 repeatable jobs: passing a custom jobId alongside repeat throws at runtime; and registering a new processor in worker/processors/index.ts changes startWorkers' booted-queue set which queue.integration.test.ts asserts verbatim.
resolution: Schedule repeatables with queue.add(name,{},{repeat:{pattern}}) — no jobId (dedup is by name+pattern). Expose the schedule via an optional ProcessorRegistration.schedule() + scheduleRepeatableJobs() called once in worker/index.ts main(). When registering a queue, update the integration test's expected booted set.

description: queue.integration.test.ts hardcoded the worker registry as [email,listing-expiry,system]. When the media wave added 'media-processing' to worker/processors/index.ts and the AI conflict-merge kept it, this assertion was never updated, so 'boots a worker for every registered queue' failed only after all Phase 1 waves landed on main (green in every isolated worktree).
resolution: Assert against the actual registry (added media-processing). Lesson: a test that hardcodes a full registry/enum is a merge-fragility hotspot — prefer asserting registeredQueues() or Object.keys(processors) so adding a processor cannot desync the test.
